questions.question: In BPE, what happens to rare or unknown words?
options: They are broken down into smaller subword units
questions.explanation: BPE breaks rare or unknown words into smaller subword units that are in its vocabulary.

questions.question: What does CBOW stand for in word2vec?
questions.explanation: CBOW stands for Continuous Bag of Words, one of the two main word2vec architectures.

questions.question: What criterion does WordPiece use to decide which token pairs to merge?
options: The pair that maximizes the likelihood of the training data
questions.explanation: WordPiece chooses the pair that maximizes the likelihood of the training data when deciding merges.

questions.question: How does WordPiece differ from BPE in selecting merges?
options: WordPiece selects merges based on likelihood, not just frequency
questions.explanation: WordPiece selects merges based on maximizing training data likelihood, not just frequency counts.

questions.question: Why is subword tokenization effective for handling large vocabularies?
options: It can represent any word by combining known subwords
questions.explanation: Subword tokenization can represent any word by combining known subwords, keeping vocabulary size manageable.

questions.question: What is the starting point for BPE training?
questions.explanation: BPE training starts with individual characters and builds up to larger units through merging.

questions.question: What is the tokenizer's vocabulary?
options: A list of all English words
A lookup table mapping tokens to unique IDs
questions.explanation: The vocabulary is a lookup table that maps each token to a unique numerical ID.

questions.question: What is the difference between token embeddings and sentence embeddings?
options: Token embeddings represent individual tokens; sentence embeddings represent entire sentences
Token embeddings only work with BERT
questions.explanation: Token embeddings represent individual tokens, while sentence embeddings represent the meaning of entire sentences.

questions.question: What does BPE stand for in tokenization?
questions.explanation: BPE stands for Byte Pair Encoding, a subword tokenization algorithm.

questions.question: What is truncation in tokenization?
options: Cutting sequences to a maximum length
questions.explanation: Truncation cuts sequences that exceed the maximum length to fit within model constraints.

questions.question: How does the length of tokenized sequences affect model performance?
options: Longer sequences require more computation and memory
questions.explanation: Longer tokenized sequences require more computation and memory, so models have maximum sequence length limits.

questions.question: Why might different tokenizers produce different tokens for the same text?
options: They have different vocabularies and training procedures
questions.explanation: Different tokenizers have different vocabularies and training procedures, so they produce different token sequences.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of tokenization in language models?
options: To translate text to other languages
To break text into smaller units that the model can process
questions.explanation: Tokenization breaks text into smaller units (tokens) that language models can process numerically.

questions.question: What does the [MASK] token do in masked language modeling?
options: Replaces words that the model needs to predict
Hides the input from the user
questions.explanation: [MASK] replaces words during masked language modeling training, and the model learns to predict the original words.

questions.question: What is the trade-off of having a very large vocabulary?
options: It increases memory requirements but may reduce sequence length
questions.explanation: A larger vocabulary requires more memory but may represent text with fewer tokens, trading memory for sequence length.

questions.question: Why is SentencePiece considered language-agnostic?
options: It translates all text to English first
It doesn't rely on language-specific pre-tokenization rules
questions.explanation: SentencePiece is language-agnostic because it doesn't rely on language-specific pre-tokenization rules like whitespace splitting.

questions.question: Can the same word always be represented by a single token?
options: No, it depends on the tokenizer and the word
questions.explanation: Whether a word maps to a single token depends on the tokenizer's vocabulary and training - rare words may be split into subwords.

questions.question: What is SentencePiece?
options: A language-agnostic tokenizer that treats space as a special character
questions.explanation: SentencePiece is a language-agnostic tokenizer that can work with any language without language-specific rules.

questions.question: How does the Skip-gram model differ from CBOW?
options: Skip-gram only works with skip lists
Skip-gram is slower and less accurate
Skip-gram predicts context words from a center word
questions.explanation: Skip-gram works in reverse of CBOW - it predicts surrounding context words given a center word.

questions.question: Which models commonly use SentencePiece tokenization?
options: T5, Llama, and many multilingual models
questions.explanation: T5, Llama, and many multilingual models use SentencePiece for tokenization.

questions.question: How is the BPE merge table created?
options: By counting frequencies and merging the most common pairs iteratively
questions.explanation: The BPE merge table is created by iteratively counting frequencies and merging the most common pairs.

questions.question: What is a subword in tokenization?
options: A unit smaller than a word but larger than a character
questions.explanation: A subword is a unit smaller than a word but larger than a character, like a morpheme or common word part.

questions.question: How do language models generate output text?
options: One token at a time autoregressively
questions.explanation: Language models generate text autoregressively, predicting one token at a time based on previous tokens.

questions.question: How do contextual embeddings differ from static embeddings like word2vec?
options: Contextual embeddings are only for images
Contextual embeddings vary based on context; static embeddings are fixed per word
Static embeddings are better for all tasks
questions.explanation: Contextual embeddings (like BERT) vary by context; static embeddings (like word2vec) have one fixed vector per word.

questions.question: What is the relationship between tokens and embeddings?
options: Tokens are converted into embeddings which are numerical vector representations
questions.explanation: Tokens are mapped to embeddings, which are dense numerical vectors that capture semantic meaning.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of BPE tokenization?
options: It handles rare words by breaking them into subword units
It is faster than other methods
questions.explanation: BPE handles rare words by breaking them into known subword units, avoiding out-of-vocabulary issues.

questions.question: What is the function of the [CLS] token in BERT?
options: To provide a classification representation for the entire input
questions.explanation: [CLS] is a special classification token whose embedding represents the entire input sequence for classification tasks.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the [PAD] token?
options: To make all sequences in a batch the same length
To mark the beginning of text
questions.explanation: The [PAD] token is added to shorter sequences to make all sequences in a batch the same length for efficient processing.

questions.question: What does a tokenizer output after processing text?
options: Token IDs that reference entries in the tokenizer's vocabulary
questions.explanation: Tokenizers output token IDs that reference specific entries in the tokenizer's vocabulary table.

questions.question: What is the main idea behind word2vec?
options: To learn word embeddings based on word co-occurrence patterns
questions.explanation: Word2vec learns word embeddings based on the idea that words appearing in similar contexts have similar meanings.

questions.question: What is a key property of word2vec embeddings?
options: Words with similar meanings have similar vector representations
questions.explanation: Word2vec produces embeddings where semantically similar words have vectors that are close in vector space.

questions.question: How does Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) work?
options: It iteratively merges the most frequent pairs of characters or tokens
It splits text at random positions
questions.explanation: BPE works by starting with characters and iteratively merging the most frequent pairs to build a vocabulary.

questions.question: What is the Unigram tokenization algorithm?
options: A method that only uses single characters
questions.explanation: Unigram is a probabilistic subword segmentation method that starts with a large vocabulary and prunes it.

questions.question: How does the CBOW model work?
options: It predicts a center word from its surrounding context words
It predicts context words from a center word
questions.explanation: CBOW predicts a target word based on its surrounding context words.

questions.question: What is the purpose of post-processing in tokenization?
options: To add special tokens and prepare the output for the model
questions.explanation: Post-processing adds special tokens (like [CLS], [SEP]) and formats the output for model consumption.

questions.question: What does the [UNK] token represent?
options: Unknown tokens not in the vocabulary
questions.explanation: [UNK] represents unknown tokens that are not present in the tokenizer's vocabulary.

questions.question: Why do we need attention masks when processing batches?
options: To make the model run faster
To prevent the model from attending to padding tokens
questions.explanation: Attention masks prevent the model from wasting computation on padding tokens that don't contain meaningful content.

questions.question: What are tokens in the context of language models?
options: The basic units of text (words, subwords, or characters) that models process
questions.explanation: Tokens are the basic units of text - they can be words, subwords, or characters depending on the tokenization method.

questions.question: What is a contextual embedding?
options: An embedding that changes based on the surrounding context
questions.explanation: Contextual embeddings change based on the surrounding words, capturing how meaning varies with context.

questions.question: What is an attention mask used for?
options: To hide tokens from the model
To tell the model which tokens are real and which are padding
questions.explanation: An attention mask indicates which tokens are real content (1) and which are padding (0) so the model knows what to attend to.

questions.question: What mathematical operation can be performed on word embeddings to find relationships?
options: Vector arithmetic (addition, subtraction) to capture semantic relationships
questions.explanation: Vector arithmetic on word embeddings can capture semantic relationships (e.g., king - man + woman ≈ queen).

questions.question: What is pre-tokenization?
options: Initial splitting of text before applying the main tokenization algorithm
questions.explanation: Pre-tokenization is the initial splitting of text (like by whitespace) before the main tokenization algorithm is applied.

questions.question: What happens to a text prompt before it reaches the language model?
questions.explanation: Text prompts are first tokenized into pieces before being processed by the language model.

questions.question: What is vocabulary size in tokenization?
options: The physical size of the tokenizer file
The number of unique tokens in the tokenizer's lookup table
The size of the training dataset
questions.explanation: Vocabulary size is the number of unique tokens in the tokenizer's lookup table.

questions.question: How does SentencePiece handle whitespace?
options: It treats space as a special character that can be tokenized
questions.explanation: SentencePiece treats space as a special character that can be tokenized, enabling language-agnostic processing.

questions.question: What is the [SEP] token used for?
options: To mark the beginning of text
To separate different segments or sentences
questions.explanation: [SEP] separates different segments of text, such as two sentences in a pair.

questions.question: What information do input IDs contain?
options: Numerical references to tokens in the vocabulary
questions.explanation: Input IDs are numerical references to tokens in the vocabulary, not the actual text characters.

questions.question: What happens when a token is out-of-vocabulary (OOV)?
options: It is replaced with the [UNK] token or subword units
questions.explanation: Out-of-vocabulary tokens are either replaced with [UNK] or broken into subword units depending on the tokenizer.

questions.question: Which tokenization algorithm was used in the original BERT model?
questions.explanation: BERT uses WordPiece tokenization, which is similar to BPE but uses likelihood-based merging.

questions.question: Why can't language models process raw text directly?
options: Models work with numerical representations, not raw characters
questions.explanation: Language models require numerical input, so raw text must be tokenized and converted to numerical representations.
